1.title: Camera Iris
1.body_md:
**Your eyes already do this.** Squinting makes distant things a little sharper — but also darker. The Camera Iris works the same way: wide open lets in more light but blurs the background; very closed keeps everything in focus but needs more light.

The side effect of a wide-open Camera Iris can be beautiful: background lights turn into soft glowing circles called *bokeh*.

images.label: Wide open — shallow focus, strong bokeh

1.metaphor_line: The sensor is a bucket — the Camera Iris is how wide the mouth opens.

2.title: Capture Duration
2.body_md:
The Camera Iris controls how *wide* the light stream is. The **Capture Duration** controls how *long* the sensor collects light.

Sports photographers use a short Capture Duration to freeze action. Night photographers use a long one to gather light trails.

A long Capture Duration makes moving subjects **streak** across the frame — we call this *motion streaking* in this lesson.
2.metaphor_line: The Capture Duration is how long the bucket sits in the rain.

3.title: Sensor Sensitivity
3.body_md:
You've opened the Camera Iris wide. The Capture Duration is as long as you can hold steady. It's still too dark.

The last option: make the sensor **more sensitive to light**. It amplifies the signal — but also amplifies noise.

Sensitivity is the last resort: a noisy photo is better than a completely dark one. You can always reduce noise in editing — you can't add light that wasn't captured.

images.label: Low — clean, needs lots of light

3.metaphor_line: Sensitivity is how big the bucket is — bigger catches more, but also more debris (noise).
